coredata.dc:title: Nanopriming with selenium doped carbon dots improved rapeseed germination and seedling salt tolerance

coredata.dc:description: Soil salinity is a big environmental issue affecting crop production. Although seed nanopriming has been widely used to improve seed germination and seedling growth under salinity, our knowledge about the underlying mechanisms is still insufficient. Herein, we newly synthesized selenium-doped carbon dots nanoparticles coated with poly acrylic acid (poly acrylic acid coated selenium doped carbon dots, PAA@Se-CDs) and used it to prime seeds of rapeseeds. The TEM (transmission electron microscope) size and zeta potential of PAA@Se-CDs are 3.8 ± 0.2 nm and −30 mV, respectively. After 8 h priming, the PAA@Se-CDs nanoparticles were detected in the seed compartments (seed coat, cotyledon, and radicle), while no such signals were detected in the NNP (no nanoparticle control) group (SeO2 was used as the NNP). Nanopriming with PAA@Se-CDs nanoparticles increased rapeseeds germination (20%) and seedling fresh weight (161%) under saline conditions compared to NNP control. PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming significantly enhanced endo-β-mannanase activities (255% increase, 21.55 µmol h−1 g−1 vs. 6.06 µmol h−1 g−1, at DAS 1 (DAS, days after sowing)), total soluble sugar (33.63 mg g−1 FW (fresh weight) vs. 20.23 mg g−1 FW) and protein contents (1.96 µg g−1 FW vs. 1.0 µg g−1 FW) to support the growth of germinating seedlings of rapeseeds under salt stress, in comparison with NNP control. The respiration rate and ATP content were increased by 76% and 607%, respectively. The oxidative damage of salinity due to the over-accumulation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) was alleviated by PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming by increasing the antioxidant enzyme activities (SOD (superoxide dismutase), POD (peroxidase), and CAT (catalase)). Another mechanism behind PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming improving rapeseeds salt tolerance at seedling stage was reducing sodium (Na+) accumulation and improving potassium (K+) retention, hence increasing the K+/Na+ ratio under saline conditions. Overall, our results not only showed that seed nanopriming with PAA@Se-CDs could be a good approach to improve salt tolerance, but also add more knowledge to the mechanism behind nanopriming-improved plant salt tolerance at germination and early seedling growth stage.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Introduction
Materials and methods
Synthesis and characterization of PAA@Se-CDs nanoparticles
Localization of PAA@Se-CDs nanoparticles in rapeseed seeds
Nanopriming, stress treatment and growth conditions
Measurement of β-mannanase activity, soluble sugar and soluble protein content at seedling growth stage
Determination of respiration rate and ATP production at seedling growth stage
Confocal imaging of ROS in the leaves of 7 d old rapeseed seedling
Measurement of MDA content and antioxidant enzymes activities
Determination of Na+, K+ and total chlorophyll content
Quantitative real-time PCR analysis
Statistical analysis
Results
PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming improved rapeseed germination and seedling growth
PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming reduced the ROS damaging effects in rapeseed leaf chloroplast and increased soluble sugar and protein contents at early seedling growth stage
PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming modulated K+/Na+ balance at seedling growth stage under saline conditions
PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming regulated β-mannanase activities and increased respiration rate and ATP production at germination and seedling growth stage
Discussion
The stimulation of Se-CDs nanopriming on antioxidant systems is a key factor to improve rapeseed salt tolerance at seedling stage
Maintaining of Na+/K+ ratio is a common indicator for nanopriming-improved seedling stage salt tolerance
Increase of endo-β-mannanase activity, respiration rate and ATP content is associated with nanopriming-improved rapeseed salt tolerance at germination and early seedling stage
Conclusions
CRediT authorship contribution statement
Acknowledgments
Supplementary data
References

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The effect of PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming on the germination and phenotype of rapeseed under saline growth conditions. (A) an image indicating the influence of PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming on the rapeseed phenotypic growth of seven days old rapeseed seedlings. (B) PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming increased rapeseed germination rate (germination rate was recorded on daily basis until 7 d after sowing) under saline growth conditions. (C–F) PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming improved seedling length, seedling establishment, fresh weight, and total chlorophyll content of 7 d old rapeseed seedlings under saline growth conditions. * indicates significant difference at P < 0.05. One batch was taken as one biological replicate (n = 3), while the error bars show the standard error of three biological replicates.

Confocal imaging of ROS level in chloroplast of rapeseed leaf under saline growth conditions. (A, B) PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming reduced the H2O2 intensity (indicated by DCF dye, green signal in chloroplast rate under saline growth conditions, and (C, D) PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming reduced the (•O2 −) intensity (indicated by DHE dye, green signal in chloroplast rate under saline growth conditions. The confocal imaging was performed for at least 3 individual samples of 7 d old rapeseed seedlings leaves. The significance between the different treatments at P < 0.05 is indicated with different letters while the error bars show the standard error of three biological replicates (n = 3). Scale bar, 25 µm.

PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming enhanced antioxidant enzymes activities, soluble sugar and protein contents to improve salt tolerance of 7 d old rapeseed seedlings. (A) PAA@Se-CDs increased SOD activity under saline growth conditions. (B) PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming increased POD activity under saline growth conditions. (C) PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming increased CAT activity under saline growth conditions. (D, E) PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming increased total soluble sugar and total soluble protein contents of 7 d old rapeseed seedlings under saline growth conditions. The significance between the different treatments at P < 0.05 is indicated with different letters on the vertical bars. One batch was taken as one biological replicate (n = 3), while the error bars show the standard error of three biological replicates.

PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming modulated K+/Na+ ratio to improve salt tolerance of 7 d old rapeseed seedlings (A, B) PAA@Se-CDs reduced Na+ content and sustained higher K+ content under saline growth conditions, and (C) PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming increased K+/Na+ ratio of 7 d old rapeseed seedlings under saline growth conditions. The significance between the different treatments at P < 0.05 is indicated with different letters on the vertical bars. One batch was taken as one biological replicate (n = 3), while the error bars show the standard error of three biological replicates.

The effect of PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming on the β-mannanase activity, respiration rate and ATP content of rapeseed seedlings at 1, 3, 5, and 7 d after sowing. (A) The influence of PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming on the activity of β-mannanase activity at 1, 3, 5, and 7 d after sowing under saline growth conditions, (B, C) Relative expression level and its ratio of β-mannanase related genes (BnMAN6) in control and PAA@SeCDs primed rapeseed seedlings at 1, 3, 5, and 7 d after sowing with/without salinity. (D, E) PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming increased respiration rate and ATP content at 1, 3, 5, and 7 d after sowing to enhance rapeseed seedlings salt tolerance. The significance between the different treatments at P < 0.05 is indicated with * or different letters. One batch was taken as one biological replicate (n = 3), while the error bars show the standard error of three biological replicates.

head.ce:title: Nanopriming with selenium doped carbon dots improved rapeseed germination and seedling salt tolerance

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Soil salinity is a big environmental issue affecting crop production. Although seed nanopriming has been widely used to improve seed germination and seedling growth under salinity, our knowledge about the underlying mechanisms is still insufficient. Herein, we newly synthesized selenium-doped carbon dots nanoparticles coated with poly acrylic acid (poly acrylic acid coated selenium doped carbon dots, PAA@Se-CDs) and used it to prime seeds of rapeseeds. The TEM (transmission electron microscope) size and zeta potential of PAA@Se-CDs are 3.8 ± 0.2 nm and −30 mV, respectively. After 8 h priming, the PAA@Se-CDs nanoparticles were detected in the seed compartments (seed coat, cotyledon, and radicle), while no such signals were detected in the NNP (no nanoparticle control) group (SeO2 was used as the NNP). Nanopriming with PAA@Se-CDs nanoparticles increased rapeseeds germination (20%) and seedling fresh weight (161%) under saline conditions compared to NNP control. PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming significantly enhanced endo-β-mannanase activities (255% increase, 21.55 µmol h−1 g−1 vs. 6.06 µmol h−1 g−1, at DAS 1 (DAS, days after sowing)), total soluble sugar (33.63 mg g−1 FW (fresh weight) vs. 20.23 mg g−1 FW) and protein contents (1.96 µg g−1 FW vs. 1.0 µg g−1 FW) to support the growth of germinating seedlings of rapeseeds under salt stress, in comparison with NNP control. The respiration rate and ATP content were increased by 76% and 607%, respectively. The oxidative damage of salinity due to the over-accumulation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) was alleviated by PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming by increasing the antioxidant enzyme activities (SOD (superoxide dismutase), POD (peroxidase), and CAT (catalase)). Another mechanism behind PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming improving rapeseeds salt tolerance at seedling stage was reducing sodium (Na+) accumulation and improving potassium (K+) retention, hence increasing the K+/Na+ ratio under saline conditions. Overall, our results not only showed that seed nanopriming with PAA@Se-CDs could be a good approach to improve salt tolerance, but also add more knowledge to the mechanism behind nanopriming-improved plant salt tolerance at germination and early seedling growth stage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: Salinity is one of the major abiotic stresses unfavorable to plant growth and development [1–3]. In salt-affected areas, salt stress creates osmotic stress around the seed, restricting seed water uptake and inhibiting or delaying seed germination [4]. The failure of the seed to uptake water under saline conditions inhibits the activities of enzymes responsible for the hydrolysis of the reserved food and the initiation of germination metabolism [5]. Moreover, the higher concentrations of sodium (Na+) and chloride (Cl−) ions in the saline soil could be toxic to seeds [6]. In recent years, seed nanopriming (prime seeds with nanomaterials) has been an emerging technique to improve seed stress tolerance [7–10]. In nanopriming, the seeds are imbibed with nanosuspensions/nanoformulations, while the nanomaterials may or may not be absorbed by the seeds [11–13]. Thus, depending on the nanomaterials used for seed nanopriming, it may positively or negatively impact seed germination. Nanopriming has been reported to aid in plant salt tolerance. For example, nanoceria (CeO2 NPs, ∼8.5 nm, ∼−43.7 mV, 0.1 mmol L−1, 8 h) were reported to enhance rapeseed salt tolerance via increasing α-amylase activities, ROS-scavenging abilities, and increasing salicylic acid (SA) levels [8,14]. Nano-silicon priming (characterization data not reported, 20 mmol L−1, 12 h) upregulated the antioxidant enzyme system to detoxify the oxidative damage and thus enhanced the salt tolerance of barley [15]. Nanopriming with iron oxide (< 50 nm, zeta potential not reported, 100 mg L−1, 10 h) increased relative water content, photosynthetic pigments, and photosynthetic rate to improve the salt tolerance of sorghum [16]. Similarly, Ag NPs nanopriming (50–100 nm, zeta potential not reported, 20 mmol L−1, 20 h) enhanced wheat salt tolerance through regulating antioxidant enzymes and metabolic pathways [17]. Thus, according to previous studies, nanopriming has certain potential to improve plant salt tolerance. Besides, nanopriming requires less amount of nanomaterials compared to soil/root or foliar application methods. For example, the estimated amount of nanoceria needed for priming rapeseed seeds for each hectare is ∼0.138 L, which is much more less than the amount needed for soil application (1000 mg kg−1) [8,18,19]. Therefore, other than enhancing plant salt tolerance, nanopriming also ensure the less use of nanomaterials while reducing the cost and biosafety concerns related to the use of nanotechnology in agriculture.

The application of carbon-based nanoparticles (CNMs) has been shown to improve seedling growth in a variety of plant species, including tomato [20] and maize [21]. Carbon dots (CDs), a new class of photoluminescent carbon nanomaterials with diameters smaller than 10 nm, have received more and more attention from researchers during the past ten years [22,23]. Moreover, CDs have been used to combat free radicals in order to prevent the cellular damage caused to macromolecules like proteins, membranes, DNA, and RNA in the cells [24,25]. Nonetheless, CDs have some drawbacks, such as low electrical conductivity, low water solubility, small surface area, and wide band gap, limiting the wide use of CDs in different fields [26–28]. To overcome these drawbacks, selenium (Se) can act as an electron withdrawing group and increase the interparticle distance between CDs. Moreover, Se is a unique trace element that is vital to many biological processes [29]. Its crucial roles in antioxidant defense and redox control have drawn much attention [30,31]. Therefore, Se-doped carbon dots (Se-CDs) have been reported to have great potential as a broad spectrum antioxidant for scavenging free radicals [31,32]. However, Se-CDs have barely been reported to improve stress tolerance in crops.

To date, the known mechanisms behind nanopriming induced improvement in seed germination and seedling growth under salinity include enhanced seed water uptake (CeO2 NPs and AgNPs), α-amylase activities (CeO2 NPs and AgNPs), soluble sugar content (CeO2 NPs and AgNPs) [7,8], regulation of ABA (Abscisic acid) /GA (Gibberellin) ratio (ZnO NPs and SeNPs) [33], regulation of SA level to scavenge ROS (CeO2 NPs) [14], regulation of antioxidant enzymes (CeO2 NPs, AgNPs, SeNPs, and ZnO NPs) [7,8,33], and regulation of ion homeostasis (ZnO NPs, SeNPs, and CeO2 NPs) [8,33]. However, the mechanism behind the nanopriming improved seed germination under stress conditions still needs more effort to be explored. For example, the effect of seed nanopriming on endo-β-mannanase activities, which is important for seed germination, is still unknown.

Rapeseed (Brassica napus L.) is an important industrial crop and is also an important oil crop [34]. However, rapeseed is moderately sensitive to salinity. Its yield is affected by salt stress more severely in arid and semi-arid areas [35]. In this study, we synthesized and characterized Se-CDs NPs coated with poly acrylic acid (PAA) and used it to prime rapeseeds to improve its salt tolerance. To the best of our knowledge, there is no report on the role of PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming in rapeseed crop under saline conditions. We assessed Se-CDs translocation in the seed coat, cotyledon, and radicle through confocal microscopy. Furthermore, we studied the role of Se-CDs nanopriming on the activities of β-mannanase, which has not been reported previously. For the hydrolysis of the β-(1–4) bonds in the mannan polymers, the enzymes responsible for catalyzing process are endo-β-mannanase (MANs; E.C. 3.2.1.78) [36,37]. In brassica, Arabidopsis, and mustard, MAN enzymes have been reported to be involved in weakening seed covering layers while facilitating the emergence of radicle during seed germination [38–40]. Thus, MAN enzymes play a role in endosperm degradation, polysaccharides break down, and reserve mobilization to begin the process of germination (radicle protrusion) [36,37]. Also, we studied whether the PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming enhances the MAN enzyme activities to produce more soluble sugar and protein contents to improve rapeseed germination and seedling growth under saline conditions. Moreover, we measured the respiration rate, ATP production, antioxidant enzyme activities, and Na+ and K+ contents to unravel the mechanism behind the PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming-induced rapeseed salt tolerance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Materials and methods
Synthesis and characterization of PAA@Se-CDs nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: 100 mg selenocysteamine hydrochloride (97%, Shanghai Yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Shanghai, China) and 50 mg m-phenylenediamine (99.5%, MACKLIN, Shanghai, China) were mixed and then dissolved in 10 mL ultra-pure water. Then, the mixture was poured into the autoclave reactor (Nanjing Zhengxing Instrument Co., PFK-100 mL, Nanjing, Jiangsu, China). Inject nitrogen into the autoclave reactor to remove oxygen. Then, the mixture solution inside the autoclave reactor was heated and stirred at 180 °C for 8 h. Then, the reacted mixture was centrifuged at 12,000 r min−1 for 1 h to collect the supernatant. The collected supernatant was named as solution A and stored at room temperature for further use. To make the solution B, 2.5 g polyacrylic acid (MW 1800, Sigma-Aldrich, Milwaukee, WI, USA) were dissolved in 5 mL ultra-pure water and stirred at 2000 r min−1 for 15 min. The obtained solutions A and B were mixed and stirred at 500 r min−1 for 24 h. The resulting mixture was placed in a 3500 Da dialysis bag for 24 h. The solution collected from the dialysis bag is the PAA@Se-CDs solution. The concentration of PAA@Se-CDs was determined by ICP-OES (Inductively coupled plasma-optical emission spectroscopy). The final solution was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C. Zeta potential was determined by 90 Plus PALS (Brookhaven Instruments, Long Island, NY, USA). 20 μL of PAA@Se-CDs was titrated on a perforated carbon-coated copper net and then imaged by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai F20, Hillsboro, Oregon) with a 200 kV accelerating voltage. Fluorescence spectrometer (F-4600, Hitachi, Japan) was used to analyze the fluorescence intensity of PAA@Se-CDs.

The photoluminescence of the PAA@Se-CDs was measured by a fluorescence spectrometer. Fig. S1A shows a significant excitation-dependent (440–510 nm) fluorescence property, and the results show that the maximum excitation wavelength of PAA@Se-CDs is about 510 nm, while the optimal emission wavelength is about 430 nm. These results indicate that the synthesized PAA@Se-CDs do not have a certain dependency on the excitation wavelength. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image shows that the size of PAA@Se-CDs nanoparticles was 3.8 ± 0.2 nm (Fig. S2A). X-ray diffraction shows that PAA@Se-CDs have a very wide diffraction peak at 2θ = 20°, which indicates that PAA@Se-CDs have an amorphous surface (Fig. S1B). The average zeta potential of the PAA@Se-CDs nanoparticles was −30 mV (Zetasizer, Nano ZS, Malvern Panalytical Ltd., Malvern, UK) (Fig. S2B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Localization of PAA@Se-CDs nanoparticles in rapeseed seeds
ce:section.ce:para: For visualizing the localization of PAA@Se-CDs nanoparticles in the seed compartments such as seed coat, cotyledon, and radicle, rapeseed seeds were immersed in the PAA@Se-CDs (50 mg L−1) solutions. At the same time, the control group rapeseed seeds were immersed in SeO2 solution. Following our previous published protocols, the seeds were kept for imbibition for 8 h at 25 ± 1 °C temperature in the dark [8]. After the imbibition period, the seeds were surface washed with DI water and surface dried with blotted papers to avoid the possible dragging of the priming solution into the seed compartments. The seeds were held in forceps and dissected into seed coat, cotyledon, and radicle (∼200 μm size) with supper thin double edge razor. The prepared slices were subsequently mounted on slides supplemented with a drop of perfluorodecalin (PFD) for good quality confocal imaging. A square coverslip was kept on the mounted samples and gently pressed to remove the air bubbles. The samples were immediately processed for confocal imaging by Leica laser scanning confocal microscope (TCS, SP8). The confocal imaging settings were 650 Gain value, 500–520 Emission light, and 448 Laser excitation light. At least 4 seeds were used as a replication of the same treatment.

After 8 h of seed nanopriming with PAA@Se-CDs, the seeds were dissected into seed coat, cotyledon, and radicle and mounted on slides to observe the uptake and distribution of PAA@Se-CDs nanoparticles in the seed coat, cotyledon, and radicle. The results from confocal imaging microscopy showed that PAA@Se-CDs nanoparticles were present in the seed coat, as indicated by the green signal (Fig. S2C). The same time, we observed a clear green signal in the cotyledon cells, indicating the presence of PAA@Se-CDs nanoparticles in the cotyledon (Fig. S2C). However, the green signal in the radicle was weak, showing that less PAA@Se-CDs nanoparticles were translocated into the radicle (Fig. S2C). No such green signals were detected in no-nanoparticles primed seed (Fig. S3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nanopriming, stress treatment and growth conditions
ce:section.ce:para: In this experiment, we used a salt-sensitive variety (Zhongshuang 11, ZS11) of rapeseed [8]. For screening the optimum concentration of PAA@Se-CDs, we used three different concentrations i.e., 25, 50, and 75 mg L−1. Based on the screening observations, 50 mg L−1 PAA@Se-CDs was the optimum concentration to improve rapeseed germination and seedling growth. The details of the screening experiments are provided in supplementary materials (Fig. S4). Briefly, seeds of ZS11 were put in PAA@Se-CDs or SeO2 (control group) solutions for 8 h. In the conical flasks, the seed-to-solution ratio was 1:5 (weight/volume). The conical flasks containing the priming solution and seeds were kept on a mechanical shaker in the dark with gentle agitation at 50 rpm. After imbibition, the seeds were surface washed with DI water and kept to dry back to their original weight. Polyethylene boxes with dimensions as 12 cm length × 12 cm breadth and × 6 cm height were used for sowing the seeds. Following our previous protocols, three sterilized germination papers were placed and moistened in the germination boxes with either 10 mL of salt solution (200 mmol L−1 NaCl dissolved in DI water, to mimic the average saline level in salinized land in China) or 10 mL of DI water [41]. Each germination box was sown with 50 seeds. The bottom two papers were replaced with new ones, and 7 mL salt solution or DI water was added to the corresponding boxes every second day. The growth chamber (Boante company, Wuhan, Hubei, China) was set at 14 h light (200 μmol m−2 s−1) and 10 h dark at 25 ± 1 and 20 ± 1 °C, respectively. The germination rate was recorded daily. The experiment was terminated after 7 d. Then, 10 uniform seedlings were selected to measure the seedling length with a ruler. Established seedlings were counted in each box based on when the seedlings had two established cotyledon leaves. From each box, all of the seedlings were collected and surface dried with blotted papers to determine the fresh weight immediately. Moreover, for soil experiments, transfer seedlings from the germination box to pots fitted with soil mix. The settings at the growth room are14 h light (200 μmol m−2 s−1) and 10 h dark at 25 ± 1 °C and 20 ± 1 °C. After two weeks, 50 mg L−1 PAA@Se-CDs were foliar sprayed on rapeseed plants. Then, after 3 h incubation, the salinity stress (pour 200 mmol L−1 NaCl solution to the pots) was onset and last for one week. Then, take photographs of the plants and collect the shoot for FW and DW measurements as described previously.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of β-mannanase activity, soluble sugar and soluble protein content at seedling growth stage
ce:section.ce:para: For β-mannanase activity, 0.1 g fresh samples were collected at DAS 1, 3, 5, and 7 and directly transferred to the liquid nitrogen tank. The activity of β-mannanase enzyme activity was estimated with a kit “β-mannanase Assay Kit” while following the manuals provided by “Solarbio Life Sciences” (Beijing Solarbio Science & Technology Co., Ltd.” kit number G0722F) [42]. To measure total soluble sugar content, about 0.1 g 7 d old whole seedlings were collected from each germination box according to the previously reported protocols [41]. The total soluble sugar content was quantified according to the manuals provided by Suzhou Biotechnology Co., Ltd. “Kit for total soluble sugar determination” (item number: G0501W) as described in previous study [8]. Similarly, according to the previously reported protocols [41], the total soluble protein content in 7 days’ fresh whole seedlings was determined using an assay kit from “Nanjing Jincheng Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (item number: A04-1–1)”. UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UV 1800PC, AOE, Shanghai, China) was used to record the readings for β-mannanase activity, soluble sugar, and soluble protein content. Three biological replicates (one batch was containing the 50 seedlings from each box, which was considered as one biological replicate) were used.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of respiration rate and ATP production at seedling growth stage
ce:section.ce:para: The small-skep method was followed to determine the respiration rate [43]. Briefly, 0.5 g germinating seedlings were collected at 1, 3, 5, and 7 DAS and were immediately put into a 0.5 L glass bottle connected with a closed-circuit system (Respirometer, model: Yaxin-1101). The fluctuation in CO2 concentration was recorded after every minute. Finally, the respiration rate was estimated based on the increase in CO2 concentration within 1 min. The ATP content was measured using “ATP Assay Kit” purchased from Solarbio Life Sciences (Beijing Solarbio Science & Technology Co., Ltd.” kit number 20220816) [43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Confocal imaging of ROS in the leaves of 7 d old rapeseed seedling
ce:section.ce:para: The dyes, 10 mol L−1 dihydroethidium (DHE, Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA) and 25 mol L−1 2′,7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (H2DCFDA, Thermo Fisher Scientific) were prepared in TES infiltration buffer (pH 7.5) to measure the in vivo contents of •O2 − and H2O2, respectively. Briefly, the leaf discs of 7 d old rapeseed seedlings were put in DCF and DHE dyes and were incubated at 25 °C for 30 min in the dark. After incubation, the samples were mounted on glass slides supplemented with a drop of perfluorodecalin (PFD) for better confocal imaging. A square coverslip was kept on the mounted samples and gently pressed to remove the air bubbles. The samples were immediately processed for confocal imaging by Leica laser scanning confocal microscope (TCS, SP8). The settings for confocal imaging were laser excitation: 488 nm, PTM1: 500–600 nm. PTM2: 700–800 nm, and Z-Stack section thickness: 4 m. whereas, 3–4 individual samples were imaged for each treatment. The fluorescence intensity of DCF and DHE was measured with LAS AF Lite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of MDA content and antioxidant enzymes activities
ce:section.ce:para: For the determination of MDA and antioxidants enzymes activities, fresh 7 d old rapeseed seedlings were collected. To estimate the content of malondialdehyde (MDA), ∼0.1 g fresh sample was homogenized with 1 mL of 5% (w/v) trichloroacetic acid at 10,000 g for 10 min. Afterward, to the 0.4 mL of the supernatant, 0.4 mL of 5% TCA (trichloroacetic acid) containing 0.67% (w/v) of TBA (thiobarbituric acid) was added. The MDA content was calculated according to previously published protocols [44]. For the determination of SOD (EC 1.15.1.1), POD (EC 1.1.1.1.7), and CAT (EC 1.11.1.6) enzymatic activities, ∼0.1 g fresh weight was ground with 1 mL of pre-cooled 50 mmol L−1 phosphate buffer (pH 7.8). Afterward, to obtain fine supernatant, the homogenate was centrifuged at 12,000×g for 20 min at 4 °C. UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UV 1800PC, AOE, Shanghai, China) was used to record reading and the activities of SOD, POD, and CAT were calculated according to the procedures of [45,46], and [47], respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Na+, K+ and total chlorophyll content
ce:section.ce:para: For the quantification of Na+ and K+, the seedlings were firstly washed with ddH2O to remove the possible adhered salts to the seedlings. Then after, the seedlings were gently dried with tissue paper. The samples were kept in an oven at 80 °C to dry until constant weight. The dry samples were finely ground in mortal and pestle, and 0.05 g was transferred to 50 mL glass tubes. The 50 mL glass tubes were added with 0.2 mL of ddH2O and 5 mL of concentrated H2SO4 (18.4 mol L−1). The samples were kept in a digestion instrument (LWY84B, Siping Institute of Electronics Technique, Siping, China). After 1.5 h, the glass tubes were added with 0.2 mL 30% H2O2, and the samples were mixed for 30 min and the samples were kept for digestion until a white smoke appeared. When the digestion was completed, the samples were taken out from the digestion instrument and put to cool down. After cooling down, the glass tubes were added with ddH2O until the solution reached the 50 mL mark. The flame photometer (FP6431, Jiangke, Shanghai, China) was set with the standard curve for Na+ and K+ determination according to the manufacturer’s instructions. The corresponding readings for Na+ and K+ were recorded according to the calculation methods explained elsewhere [48].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative real-time PCR analysis
ce:section.ce:para:
Fast Plant RNA Kit for Polysaccharides & Polyphenolics-Rich (#ZP411C-1, ZOMANBIO) was used for RNA (ribonucleic acid) extraction. ToloScript All-in-one RT EasyMix for qPCR (#22107, Tolo Biotech., Ltd., Shanghai, China) was used for reverse transcription to obtain cDNA. After reverse transcription, the quality of reverse transcription was measured by reference genes. Primers and cDNA that could amplify the reference gene bands were selected for the next experiment. Primers and cDNAs that can amplify reference gene bands were selected for the next experiment. Real-time fluorescence quantitative polymerase chain reaction (qPCR) was performed with 2× Q3 SYBR qPCR Master Mix (Universal) (#22204, Tolo Biotech., Ltd.). PCR procedure was: 94 °C pre-denaturation for 30 s, followed by 40 cycles of 94 °C denaturations for 5 s, 56 °C annealing for 30 s and 72 °C extensions for 10 s. Expression analysis was calculated using 2−ΔΔ
C
T as described in previous studies [8,14]. The design of used primers was done by using NCBI Primer-BLAST (https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/tools/primer-blast/). The used primers are shown in Table S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Statistical analysis
ce:section.ce:para: One-way ANOVA (Ducan test) and the Independent-Samples t-Test in SPSS software were used to compare means. Non-parametric tests based on One-Sample K-S (Kolmogorov-Smirnov test) were used to test the normal distribution of all data. * indicates P < 0.05. Different lowercase letters represent significance at P < 0.05. Standard errors are shown by error bars. Excel 2016 was used to create the graphs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results
PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming improved rapeseed germination and seedling growth
ce:section.ce:para: The influence of nanopriming with PAA@Se-CDs nanoparticles on the phenotype of rapeseed seedling growth under salt stress (200 mmol L−1 NaCl) is shown in Fig. 1 A. Seed nanopriming with PAA@Se-CDs nanoparticles significantly increased rapeseed germination by 20% as compared to the control group (91% vs. 75%, respectively) under saline conditions (Fig. 1B). Salt stress decreased seedling length; however, PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming increased seedling growth by 107% compared with NNP priming (6.2 cm vs. 2.7 cm, respectively) (Fig. 1C). Compared with the control group, PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming increased the number of established seedlings by 97% (82% vs. 38%, respectively) (Fig. 1D). Moreover, PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming significantly increased the fresh weight of rapeseed seedlings by 161% compared to the control group (2.21 g/box vs. 0.90 g/box, respectively) (Fig. 1E). There was no significant effect of the priming treatments on the chlorophyll content of rapeseed seedlings under non-saline conditions (Fig. 1F). Overall, saline growth significantly reduced the total chlorophyll content of rapeseed seedlings. Nonetheless, PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming established seedlings had 403% higher total chlorophyll content than NPPs priming treatment under saline conditions. Indeed, PAA@Se-CDs treated rapeseed plants (grown in soil) showed better phenotypic performance, and higher FW (1.2 ± 0.06 vs. 0.8 ± 0.10 g plant−1) and DW (0.129 ± 0.010 vs. 0.078 ± 0.011 g plant−1) than control plants under salinity stress (200 mmol L−1 NaCl, 7 d) (Fig. S5).

ce:section.ce:section-title: PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming reduced the ROS damaging effects in rapeseed leaf chloroplast and increased soluble sugar and protein contents at early seedling growth stage
ce:section.ce:para: For the confocal microscopy observation of the intensity of H2O2 in the chloroplast of rapeseed cotyledon leaf, H2DCFDA dye (ROS indicator that converts to its fluorescent DCF form upon reaction with ROS) was used. While for the confocal microscopy observation of •O2 − intensity in the chloroplast of rapeseed cotyledon leaf, fluorescent product 2-hydroxyethidium dye (DHE, its fluorescence increases after reacting with •O2 −) was used. Under non-saline conditions, the fluorescent intensity of ROS (H2O2 and •O2 −, as indicated by the green signal of DCF and DHE dyes, respectively, in the chloroplast) was weak. There were no significant differences between NNP priming and PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming for the ROS intensities under non-saline conditions (Fig. S6). However, saline conditions significantly increased the intensities of H2O2 and •O2 − in the chloroplasts, indicating the salt-induced damaging effect on chloroplast (Fig. 2 A–C). The DCF fluorescent intensity (H2O2, indicator) was 61% higher in NNP priming treatment than PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming (Fig. 2A, B). Similarly, DHE fluorescent intensity (•O2 −, indicator) was 49% higher in NNP priming treatment than PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming (Fig. 2B, C). These results revealed that PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming greatly reduced the ROS production in the chloroplast of rapeseed cotyledon leaf. Due to the remarkable reduction in ROS production, PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming significantly reduced the MDA content by 36% compared with NNPs priming (6980 nmol g−1 FW vs. 10851 nmol g−1 FW, respectively) under saline conditions (Fig. 2D). Moreover, PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming increased the antioxidant enzymes activities, including SOD, POD, and CAT, by 68%, 31%, and 25%, respectively, under saline conditions compared with NNPs priming (Fig. 3 A–C). However, there were no significant differences in SOD, POD, and CAT activities between NNPs priming and PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming under non-saline conditions (Fig. 3A–C). The priming treatments had no significant effect on the total soluble sugar and total soluble protein contents under non-saline conditions (Fig. 4 D, E). However, salt stress significantly increased the total soluble sugar and protein contents. Compared to NNPs priming, seed nanopriming with PAA@Se-CDs nanoparticles significantly increased total soluble sugar content by 65% (33.63 mg g−1 FW vs. 20.23 mg g−1 FW, respectively) and total soluble protein content by 88.79% (1.96 µg g−1 FW vs. 1.0 µg g−1 FW, respectively) under saline conditions (Fig. 3D, E).

ce:section.ce:section-title: PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming modulated K+/Na+ balance at seedling growth stage under saline conditions
ce:section.ce:para: Not surprisingly, more Na+ content was recorded in rapeseed seedlings grown under saline conditions compared with non-saline conditions (Fig. 4A). However, under saline conditions, the Na+ content was significantly lower by 64% in the seedlings established from PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming treatment compared with the seedlings established from NNPs priming established seedlings (Fig. 4A). There was no significant effect of the priming treatments on K+ content under non-saline conditions (Fig. 4B). As usual, saline conditions resulted in the depletion of K+ content compared with non-saline conditions (Fig. 4B). Interestingly, under saline conditions, PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming established seedlings maintained 175% higher K+ content compared with NNPs priming established (Fig. 4B). Due to the remarkable changes in the Na+ and K+ contents, PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming better maintained K+/Na+ ratio under saline conditions (Fig. 4C). Under saline conditions, PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming maintained a higher K+/Na+ by 654% compared with NNPs priming (Fig. 4C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming regulated β-mannanase activities and increased respiration rate and ATP production at germination and seedling growth stage
ce:section.ce:para: Under control conditions, β-mannanase enzymatic activity was higher for DAS 1, while a decrease in β-mannanase enzymatic activity was recorded for DAS 3, 5, and DAS 7 (Fig. 5 A). There was no significant difference between no-nanoparticles priming (NNPs) and PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming in β-mannanase enzymatic activity under non-saline conditions for DAS 1, DAS 3, DAS 5, and DAS 7 (Fig. 5A). Overall, salt stress decreased the enzymatic activity of β-mannanase during seed germination and seedling growth (Fig. 5A). Surprisingly, PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming significantly increased the β-mannanase enzymatic activity under salt stress compared with the control group. The peak increase in β-mannanase enzymatic activity due to PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming was observed at DAS 1 (255% increase, 21.55 µmol h−1 g−1 vs. 6.06 µmol h−1 g−1) compared to NNPs priming (Fig. 5A). However, PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming sustained an increase in β-mannanase enzymatic activity by 72% and 12% at DAS 3, and DAS 5, respectively, with a slight decrease (1%) at DAS 7, as compared to NNPs priming. qPCR results showed that compared with control plants under salinity, the relative expression level of BnMAN6 in PAA@Se-CDs primed rapeseeds was significantly decreased from DAS 1–7 (Fig. 5B). While, compared with control condition, only the ratio of relative expression level of BnMAN6 (not the other genes such as BnMAN1, BnMAN2, BnMAN5, and BnMAN7 (Fig. S7)), between control plants and PAA@Se-CDs primed rapeseeds under salinity was significantly increased at DAS3 (0.7178 vs. 0.8585, 19.6%) (Fig. 5C). These results showed that the BnMAN6 is the most responsive gene for PAA@Se-CDs improved β-mannanase activities in rapeseeds than control under salinity. While Both under non-saline and saline conditions, the respiration rate was increased with DAS (1, 3, 5, and 7) (Fig. 5D). Under non-saline conditions, there was no significant difference between NNP priming and PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming in respiration rate at different DAS. However, under saline conditions, PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming increased respiration rate by 76%, 144%, 69%, and 52% at DAS 1, 3, 5, and 5, respectively (Fig. 5D). Moreover, compared with NNP priming, PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming increased ATP content by 607%, 149%, 92%, and 69% at DAS 1, 3, 5, and 5, respectively (Fig. 5E). Nonetheless, across the DAS, NNP priming and PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming had no significant effect on the ATP content under non-saline conditions (Fig. 5E).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discussion
The stimulation of Se-CDs nanopriming on antioxidant systems is a key factor to improve rapeseed salt tolerance at seedling stage
ce:section.ce:para: Under normal conditions, the uptake of water by seeds triggers a series of biological reactions in which the metabolism of the endosperm provides rapid energy for the growth and development of the germinating seedlings [49–51]. However, salinity resulted in osmotic stress, thus restricting seed water uptake, which either completely inhibits seed germination or increases the time needs for seed germination [2,52,53]. The decrease in the germination ability, seedling growth, and biomass accumulation due to salinity has been reported in different crop species, including rice [54], maize [55], wheat [52], soybean [56], sorghum [57], and sunflower [58]. Similar results were also shown in this study. The slower daily germination rate significantly reduced the seedling growth, seedling establishment, and seedling biomass and chlorophyll content in the control treatment under saline conditions. Thus, similar to the other plant species, rapeseed also exhibited salt sensitivity, as evidenced by reduced germination rate and seedling growth. The sensitivity of rapeseed to salinity stress during germination and seedling growth stage has been reported by previous studies [35,59]. Not surprisingly, in this study, we found that PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming improved rapeseed seedlings salt tolerance, which was indicated by 107%, 98%, 161%, and 403% increases in seedling length, seedling establishment, fresh weight, and chlorophyll content, respectively, compared to NNP treatment under 200 mmol L−1 NaCl (Fig. 1B–F). This is similar to previous studies. For example, Se NPs and ZnO NPs were reported to increase rapeseed germination and seedling growth under salt stress [33]. Seed nanopriming with cerium oxide nanoparticles (CeO2 NPs) has also been reported to improve rapeseed salt tolerance at the germination stage [8,14]. Compared to hydropriming, seed priming with ZnO NPs was reported to increase the germination rate of wheat [60]. AgNO3 NPs priming was reported to improve the germination ability of aged rice seeds [7]. Similarly, nZVI (zero-valent iron) nanopriming was reported to increase the germination rate of rice [61]. The findings of the previous studies and the results of the present study suggest that nanopriming is an effective approach to improving the germination rate of different crop species under stresses, including salinity. However, our knowledge about the mechanisms behind how nanopriming improves seed germination under salinity remains insufficient.

The over-accumulation of ROS is one of the reasons behind the failure of seed germination and seedling emergence under salinity stress [62–64]. For example, the over-accumulation of H2O2, •O2 −, and MDA inhibited the seed germination and seedling growth of Leymus chinensis under salt-alkali stress [65]. In cauliflower, salt stress increased oxidative damage and adversely affected seedling growth [66]. Similarly, the production of ROS under salt stress inhibited rapeseed seedling growth [14]. This is the same case in the present study, showing increased ROS level in rapeseed under salinity. While seed nanopriming with PAA@Se-CDs posed the ability to reduce ROS level and alleviate oxidative damage in rapeseed under salt stress. This is in accordance with previous studies that nanopriming could increase ROS scavenging abilities in seeds and seedlings under salinity [8,14]. Plants with better antioxidant enzymes always show better salt tolerance [2,67,68]. In the current study, the increase in SOD (68%), POD (31%), and CAT (25%) activities was significantly higher in PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming treatment compared to NNP treatment (Fig. 3A–C). Thus, the significant increase in the antioxidant enzyme activities in PAA@Se-CDs nanoprimed treatment might have reduced oxidative damage and improved rapeseed salt tolerance. In wheat, seed nanopriming with chitosan and green tea nanocomposite increased the antioxidant enzyme activities to reduce oxidative damage and improve salt tolerance [69]. Seed treatment with CeO2 NPs enhanced the ROS scavenging abilities by upregulating the antioxidant defense system while better enabling cotton salt tolerance [9]. Also, CeO2 NPs nanopriming enhanced rapeseed salt tolerance by upregulating the antioxidant enzyme activities [8]. In rice seedlings, the CeO2 NPs greatly modulated the antioxidant enzyme activities to reduce oxidative damage [70]. TiO2 NPs increased the germination rate of peas by enhancing the antioxidant enzyme system [71]. Similarly, seed nanopriming with TiO2 NPs improved maize salt tolerance while increasing the SOD, POD, and CAT enzymatic activities [72]. Overall, it is obvious that nanopriming maintains better ROS homeostasis and can improve plant salt tolerance at germination and seedling growth stages.

Under stress conditions, the amount of soluble sugars and proteins increases the germination rate and improves plant stress tolerance [6,52,73]. Moreover, soluble sugars and proteins are compatible solutes important for maintaining osmotic balance under salt stress [2,53]. These compatible solutes stabilize proteins and their complexes, as well as membrane stability under salinity stress, by reducing the effect of the high ion concentrations (Na+ and Cl−) on the activity of enzymes [74,75]. Recently, nanomaterials have been reported to enhance plant salt tolerance by increasing the levels of soluble solutes, including soluble proteins and sugars [74,76]. Here, our results show that seed nanopriming with PAA@Se-CDs increased the soluble sugar and protein contents under the saline conditions (Fig. 3D, E). Our previous study reported that seed nanopriming with CeO2 NPs increased seed water uptake, enhancing α-amylase activities and soluble sugar production to improve rapeseed seed germination under salt stress [8]. Similarly, rice seed nanopriming with AgNO3 NPs significantly increased the soluble sugar content to support the rice seedling growth [7]. ZnO nanopriming was also reported to increase the contents of soluble sugar and protein to alleviate salinity stress in lupine [77]. Polyhydroxy fullerenes nanoparticles (PHF NPs) nanopriming increased soluble sugars and other osmolytes to enhance the salt tolerance of wheat [78]. Similarly, single-walled carbon nanohorns (SWCNHs) and ZnO NPs increased the contents of soluble sugars and proteins to improve the salt tolerance of Sophora alopecuroides [79]. It shows that the increase in soluble sugar and protein contents due to nanopriming facilitates seedling growth under salinity stress.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Maintaining of Na+/K+ ratio is a common indicator for nanopriming-improved seedling stage salt tolerance
ce:section.ce:para: Salt stress increases the uptake and accumulation of Na+ while depleting K+ ions from plants [2,80,81]. Thus, plants with good ability to sustain the balance between Na+ and K+ ions are usually salt tolerant [81–83]. In the current study, salt stress significantly increased the Na+ accumulation in the 7 d old rapeseed seedlings. However, PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming treatment reduced the Na+ accumulation (64%) in rapeseed seedlings compared to the NNP control group (Fig. 4A). These results exhibit that PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming helped to reduce the over-accumulation of Na+ to improve rapeseed salt tolerance. Similar results were reported in maize, where seed nanopriming reduced the Na+ accumulation in maize seedlings under salt stress [72]. Ag NPs nanopriming was also reported to reduce Na+ accumulation and improve pearl millet salt tolerance [17]. Seed treatment with CeO2 NPs reduced the Na+ accumulation in rapeseed under saline conditions [8]. Nonetheless, salt stress reduced the K+ content in the rapeseed seedlings under saline conditions. Interestingly, PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming sustained 175% higher K+ content in rapeseed seedlings compared to the NNP group (Fig. 4B). It is well known that under salt stress, the ability to maintain K+ is associated with plant salt tolerance [2,82,84]. Overall, compared with NNP group, PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming maintained a higher K+/Na+ ratio in rapeseed seedlings grown under salt stress (Fig. 4C). This is similar with previous studies, reporting that seed nanopriming with different nanomaterials enhances plant salt tolerance through better maintaining K+/Na+ ratio under salinity stress [8,9,72].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Increase of endo-β-mannanase activity, respiration rate and ATP content is associated with nanopriming-improved rapeseed salt tolerance at germination and early seedling stage
ce:section.ce:para: To identify the mechanism behind why PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming increases seed germination, we determined the activity of the endo-β-mannanase enzyme in the germinating seeds under salt stress. The endo-β-mannanase enzyme weakens the seed coat to facilitate the protrusion of radicle and mobilization of reserves from the endosperm, supporting the seedling growth until the plant becomes fully photosynthetic [39,42,62]. In the current study, our results showed that under non-saline growing conditions, the endo-β-mannanase enzyme activity was at the highest peak on DAS 1, while for the DAS 3, 5, and 7 was decreased (Fig. 5A). However, salinity stress inhibited the endo-β-mannanase enzyme activity in NNP treatment compared to non-saline conditions. Interestingly, PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming recorded the highest peak increase in endo-β-mannanase enzyme activity by 255% and 73% at DAS 1 and DAS 2, respectively, compared NNP treatment under saline conditions. Therefore, the early endo-β-mannanase enzyme activity due to PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming could be one of the mechanisms behind higher rapeseed germination under salinity stress. However, the role of nanopriming in regulating the endo-β-mannanase enzyme activity has rarely been reported. Drought stress inhibited the endo-β-mannanase enzyme activity in lettuce, and only 4% seed germination was recorded [85]. Similarly, in Brachypodium distachyon, the recorded endo-β-mannanase enzyme activity showed a peak at 24 h in the germinating seeds [86]. In tomato, higher enzymatic activity of the endo-β-mannanase enzyme was reported in transgenic tomato plant compared to the control line during 20 h of imbibition [87]. Thus, regulating the endo-β-mannanase enzyme plays an important role in seed germination.

The early stages of seed germination need higher O2 concentration, evidenced by the higher respiration rate [88]. During seed germination, rapid respiration, i.e., higher O2 uptake and CO2 release, occurs [88,89]. The respiration rate produces ATP, providing vital energy for the growth and development of the germinating seedlings [89–91]. Excessive salts affect seed germination by disrupting respiration rate and ATP production [63,92,93]. The present study showed that salt stress reduced the respiration rate and ATP contents in the rapeseed germinating seedlings (Fig. 5D, E). However, compared with NNP priming group, seed nanopriming with PAA@Se-CDs increased respiration rate and ATP production. The respiration rate and increased ATP production are associated with stress tolerance during germination [43,53,88]. Therefore, rapeseed salt tolerance at the germination stage might be associated with a higher respiration rate and ATP contents due to seed nanopriming with PAA@Se-CDs. The improvement in rice stress tolerance due to priming was ascribed to the high respiration rate, enabling rapid endosperm metabolism and providing more energy for the germinating seeds [43]. Similarly, seed nanopriming with AgNPs enhanced wheat respiration intensity and seed vigor index [94,95].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusions
ce:section.ce:para: Stress always limits plant growth [96]. Seed nanopriming, which has environmental and economic advantages (low usage of nanomaterials dosage, less biosafety concerns, and low cost), is an emerging approach to improve plant salt tolerance at seedling growth stage. However, our knowledge about the mechanisms behind nanopriming improved salt tolerance at germination and early seedling growth stages are still insufficient. To the best of our knowledge, the role and the behind mechanisms of PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming in improving plant salt tolerance has rarely been reported, especially in rapeseed at germination and seedling growth stages. PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming rapidly increased the activity of the endo-β-mannanase enzyme, resulting in a higher rapeseed germination rate under salinity. Subsequently, the higher total soluble sugar and protein contents and ATP production were found in PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming improved rapeseed salt tolerance. PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming also exhibited better ROS scavenging abilities through upregulating antioxidant enzyme activities. Furthermore, PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming increased the K+/Na+ ratio under saline conditions. Overall, the findings of our study provide more insights into the mechanisms behind PAA@Se-CDs nanopriming-induced plant salt tolerance at germination stage.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:section-title: Supplementary data
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data for this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cj.2024.03.007.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:section-title: Supplementary data
ce:section.ce:para:
